video_title: The Surprising Truth About Who's Buying Bitcoin in Europe
gist: Julian Liniger, founder/CEO of the self-custodial Bitcoin exchange Relay, describes Europe's Bitcoin buyers as older and more affluent than expected (not young digital natives), details how the EU's new MiCA license reshaped market access, and argues the current bear market's retail apathy stems from European cost-of-living pressure and capital chasing AI/big-tech hype rather than Bitcoin disbelief, with a bottom already in and a new bull market expected in 2027.

insights.claim: Relay, a Bitcoin-only self-custodial exchange founded in Switzerland in 2020, has onboarded more than 100,000 Europeans who have collectively bought around 20,000 Bitcoin through the platform over six years.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Signals meaningful organic European retail on-ramp adoption for Bitcoin outside the major custodial exchanges, a proxy data point for regional retail demand.
insights.quote: we've onboarded more than 100,000 Europeans to Bitcoin, and they bought around 20,000 Bitcoin with us in the last 6 years

insights.claim: Unlike custodial exchanges such as Coinbase or Bitpanda, Relay never holds a penny of user funds, instead integrating a self-custodial wallet directly into its exchange so users always control their own keys.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Differentiates Relay's counterparty-risk profile from custodial competitors, relevant when assessing exchange/platform risk in the European Bitcoin on-ramp market.

insights.claim: Liniger believes self-custodial Bitcoin will become easier to use than custodial financial services within 5 to 10 years, as tools like cloud backup and face-ID recovery eliminate the need to manage seed phrases while custodial providers face growing regulatory compliance burdens.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: If self-custody UX converges with or beats custodial convenience, it could accelerate disintermediation of custodial exchanges and ETFs as the default retail access point for Bitcoin.
insights.quote: it will be actually in the self-custodial way it's going to be easier to interact with Bitcoin than in custodial way

themes.theme: Who's Actually Buying Bitcoin in Europe

insights.claim: Relay's actual core customer base skews toward people aged 40 to 80 who are not tech- or finance-savvy (e.g., taxi drivers, hairdressers, shop workers), contradicting the company's original assumption that its users would be young, digital-native, anti-establishment millennials in their 20s and early 30s.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Suggests the addressable market for Bitcoin retail on-ramps is broader and older than typically assumed, which matters for sizing future adoption curves and marketing strategy in the space.
insights.quote: He lives on the countryside in Germany. He's 43 years old.

insights.claim: Some of Relay's more affluent, older private clients started investing a few hundred dollars and have scaled up to allocations of 10,000, 100,000, or even millions of dollars in Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Indicates a wealth-accumulation pathway among retail Bitcoin buyers in Europe, useful signal for private banks and wealth platforms assessing client demand for Bitcoin exposure.

insights.claim: Germany makes up more than 50% of Relay's user base, with Switzerland, Austria, Italy, and France following as the next largest markets, while Eastern and Northern Europe remain comparatively small despite no active marketing being needed to drive the initial German-speaking growth.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Maps the concentration of European Bitcoin retail demand geographically, relevant for competitors or investors evaluating regional expansion priorities.

insights.claim: Bitcoin adoption rate is roughly 20% in Switzerland versus about 10-15% in Germany and under 10% in most other European countries, which Liniger attributes partly to cultural trust in local/Swiss brands rather than purely to financial-system stability.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Provides a rare country-level adoption-rate benchmark for Bitcoin in Europe that can anchor forecasts of total addressable market by country.
insights.quote: the adoption rate in Switzerland is like 20% which is ahead of the average 10% 10 15% for example in Germany and 10 or less percent in other European countries

insights.claim: The EU's MiCA (Markets in Crypto-Assets) license, which Relay obtained by the end of 2025, is described as nearly equivalent to a banking license and was mandatory for any firm to keep offering crypto brokerage services to EU clients past the compliance deadline this month, forcing unlicensed competitors to halt EU operations entirely.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: MiCA functions as a high barrier to entry that could consolidate the European crypto exchange market around a small number of licensed players like Relay, a competitive-dynamics signal for the sector.

insights.claim: Liniger believes EU politicians and traditional media push negative Bitcoin narratives (volatility, crime, energy use, quantum risk) partly to keep citizens holding euros, which are subject to high inflation and taxation of up to 50% income tax plus unrealized gains, capital gains, and wealth taxes.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Frames EU regulatory and media hostility toward Bitcoin as a structural, incentive-driven headwind rather than a purely technical concern, relevant to expectations for how fast policy friction eases.
insights.quote: most EU countries take like up to 50% income tax and then there's unrealized gain tax and capital gain tax

themes.theme: Bear Market Psychology and Retail Behavior

insights.claim: Despite the fear and greed index sitting at absolute fear during this bear market, Relay retains almost all of its recurring Bitcoin savings-plan (DCA) subscribers, showing that educated long-term holders are not capitulating even as one-time discretionary buying dries up.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Sticky DCA behavior among educated holders suggests a resilient floor of demand independent of sentiment swings, useful for gauging structural versus speculative Bitcoin demand.

insights.claim: Relay consistently sees roughly an 80/20 buy-to-sell ratio among users, with buy-the-dip volume spiking three to five times the average on days Bitcoin drops 4-5%, indicating dip-buying dominates over panic-selling on its platform.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: A concrete, platform-level buy/sell ratio investors can use as a sentiment proxy for retail dip-buying conviction during drawdowns.

insights.claim: According to Liniger, no Relay user who has run a Bitcoin savings plan for at least four years has ever lost money, reinforcing his framing of DCA as a reliably profitable long-term strategy on the platform's data.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: A specific, testable claim about DCA outcomes that investors could use to evaluate the historical robustness of a multi-year Bitcoin dollar-cost-averaging strategy.
insights.quote: No one has ever lost money that has uh done a savings plan for 4 years

insights.claim: In contrast to the two prior bear markets Relay has been through, this one is marked by unusual retail apathy rather than active trading or panic, which Liniger attributes to European cost-of-living pressure (rising rent, food, insurance, taxes) leaving people with less money and attention for Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Ties depressed retail engagement to macro household-budget stress rather than lost conviction in Bitcoin, implying pent-up demand could return once real incomes stabilize.

insights.claim: European retail capital that might otherwise go to Bitcoin is instead chasing the AI/big-tech hype (Google, Amazon, Meta, Nvidia) and high-profile IPOs like SpaceX, reflecting a short-term, gambling-driven search for fast returns rather than long-term dollar-cost-averaging discipline.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Identifies AI-stock and mega-IPO mania as a direct competitor for the same pool of speculative retail capital that would otherwise flow into Bitcoin, a rotation dynamic to watch as the AI trade matures.
insights.quote: They invest rather in in the big tech, Google, Amazon, you know, Meta, etc., Nvidia.

insights.claim: Citing Michael Saylor, Liniger argues most people who believe Bitcoin is 'too late' after its prior 100x simply haven't invested the roughly 100 hours needed to genuinely understand it, whereas those who do usually end up starting a long-term savings plan.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Frames retail non-participation as an education/time-investment gap rather than a valid valuation objection, a lens for evaluating why adoption lags despite bullish fundamentals.
insights.quote: You need to put in at least 100 hours.

themes.theme: Macro Bitcoin Outlook and Capital Rotation

insights.claim: Liniger states Relay saw a roughly 56% drawdown in Bitcoin price and a clear bottom in user activity and sentiment in April, with improvement in May and a strong rebound in user activity starting in June.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Provides a platform-level, activity-based timestamp for the cycle bottom that can be cross-checked against price and on-chain data for cycle-timing purposes.
insights.quote: We have though seen a bottom in April, clearly.

insights.claim: Liniger expects Bitcoin to stay relatively stable to modestly higher through the rest of this year without reaching $100k, because AI buildout and big-tech IPOs are absorbing hundreds of billions of dollars in liquidity and geopolitical war uncertainty continues to weigh on risk appetite.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Sets a near-term price ceiling expectation ($100k unreached this year) tied explicitly to AI-driven liquidity absorption as the mechanism, a thesis investors can monitor via AI capex and IPO flow data.

insights.claim: Liniger predicts that once the AI investment cycle matures ('bubbles out') and investors realize gains next year, freed-up capital will rotate into hard assets including Bitcoin, driving a new bull market with all-time highs well above $100k in the following year.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Articulates a specific capital-rotation catalyst (AI trade maturing → profit-taking → reallocation to hard assets) that investors could track as a leading indicator for the next Bitcoin bull leg.
insights.quote: there will be a lot of free capital that will need to flow somewhere

insights.claim: Liniger's core long-term thesis is that Bitcoin's fixed supply makes it structurally unique versus every other asset class, since stocks, gold, and oil can all be created or produced in greater quantity, positioning Bitcoin as the ultimate long-term store of value as global asset supply expands.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Restates the core scarcity thesis underpinning Liniger's (and Relay's) long-term bullishness, the anchor assumption behind his multi-year price and adoption forecasts.
insights.quote: It's the only thing that it's just fixed supply.

insights.claim: Relay's stated long-term goal is to eventually go public, betting that Bitcoin's brand recognition (comparable to Coca-Cola or Apple) combined with the company's own Bitcoin treasury holdings creates a flywheel where rising Bitcoin prices simultaneously boost company valuation, client asset values, and trading volume/revenue.
insights.speaker: Julian Liniger, CEO of Relay
insights.so_what: Signals a future European Bitcoin-exchange IPO candidate whose valuation is explicitly levered to Bitcoin price appreciation, worth tracking as a potential public listing tied to Bitcoin's cycle.
